name: CBAC (6-State Governance Circuit Breaker)
overview: CBAC is a 6-state governance circuit breaker that controls all data access transitions using Compare-And-Swap (CAS) semantics. Unlike traditional RBAC which filters data after loading it into memory, CBAC determines whether a page can even be decrypted — access control is enforced at the key derivation layer, making unauthorized access mathematically impossible rather than policy-filtered. 43 tests including 23 adversarial gym scenarios verify these guarantees under attack.

howItWorks.title: State Machine
howItWorks.description: Each governed entity has a CBAC state: Open, Restricted, Sealed, Quarantined, Revoked, or Archived. State transitions are atomic via CAS (compare-and-swap) — no partial transitions, no race conditions.

howItWorks.title: Key Derivation (RDKC)
howItWorks.description: The CBAC state feeds into RDKC (governance-bound key derivation). Each page gets a unique AES-256-GCM-SIV key derived from 4 governance inputs: clearance level, CBAC state, entity classification, and temporal epoch. Change any input → different key → old key cannot decrypt.

howItWorks.title: Access Check
howItWorks.description: Before any read or write, the engine checks: does the requestor's clearance + entity's CBAC state produce a valid key? If not, the operation fails at the cryptographic layer — the data is never loaded into memory.

howItWorks.title: Audit Trail
howItWorks.description: Every CBAC state transition is recorded with timestamp, actor, previous state, and new state. This creates a tamper-evident governance history.

comparisons.source: No competitor has adversarial access control gym

comparisons.narrative: Traditional databases enforce access control by loading data first, then filtering. TrustDB enforces access at the key derivation layer — if you don't have the right clearance + CBAC state, you can't derive the decryption key. The data never enters memory.

competitors.approach: Row-Level Security policies filter rows after loading from disk
competitors.limitation: Data is in memory before filtering — side-channel attacks possible

competitors.approach: Sub-graph access control via property rules
competitors.limitation: Policy-based, not cryptographic; admin can bypass

competitors.approach: Field-Level Encryption with app-layer key management
competitors.limitation: Keys managed by application, not derived from governance state

competitors.approach: Separate authorization service with set operations
competitors.limitation: Network hop for every check; not storage-integrated
